elements.sent-2411-001: The densest subgraph problem is a classic problem in combinatorial optimisation.
elements.sent-2411-002: Graphs with low maximum subgraph density are often called ``uniformly sparse'', leading to algorithms parameterised by this density.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, in reality, the sparsity of a graph is not necessarily uniform.
elements.sent-2411-004: This calls for a formally well-defined, fine-grained notion of density.
elements.sent-2411-005: Danisch, Chan, and Sozio propose a definition for local density that assigns to each vertex $v$ a value $\rho^*(v)$.
elements.sent-2411-006: This local density is a generalisation of the maximum subgraph density of a graph.
elements.sent-2411-007: i.e., if $\rho(G)$ is the subgraph density of a finite graph $G$, then $\rho(G)$ equals the maximum local density $\rho^*(v)$ over vertices $v$ in $G$.
elements.sent-2411-008: They present a Frank-Wolfe-based algorithm to approximate the local density of each vertex with no theoretical (asymptotic) guarantees.
elements.sent-2411-009: We provide an extensive study of this local density measure.
elements.sent-2411-010: Just as with (global) maximum subgraph density, we show that there is a dual relation between the local out-degrees and the minimum out-degree orientations of the graph.
elements.sent-2411-011: We introduce the definition of the local out-degree $\gee^*(v)$ of a vertex $v$, and show it to be equal to the local density $\rho^*(v)$.
elements.sent-2411-012: We consider the local out-degree to be conceptually simpler, shorter to define, and easier to compute.
elements.sent-2411-013: Using the local out-degree we show a previously unknown fact: that existing algorithms already dynamically approximate the local density for each vertex with polylogarithmic update time.
elements.sent-2411-014: Next, we provide the first distributed algorithms that compute the local density with provable guarantees: given any $\varepsilon$ such that $\varepsilon^{-1 \in O(\operatorname{poly n)$, we show a deterministic distributed algorithm in the LOCAL model where, after $O(\varepsilon^{-2 \log^2 n)$ rounds, every vertex $v$ outputs a $(1 + \varepsilon)$-approximation of their local density $\rho^*(v)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: In CONGEST, we show a deterministic distributed algorithm that requires $\text{poly (\log n,\varepsilon^{-1 ) \cdot 2^{O(\sqrt{\log n )}$ rounds, which is sublinear in $n$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: As a corollary, we obtain the first deterministic algorithm running in a sublinear number of rounds for $(1+\varepsilon)$-approximate densest subgraph detection in the CONGEST model.
elements.sent-2411-017: Density or sparsity measures of graphs are widely studied and have many applications.
elements.sent-2411-018: Examples include the arboricity, the degeneracy, and the maximum subgraph density, all of which are asymptotically related within a factor of $2$.
elements.sent-2411-019: Given a graph or subgraph $H$, its density, $\rho(H)$, is the average number of edges per vertex in $H$.
elements.sent-2411-020: The maximum subgraph density $\rho^{\max (G)$ of a graph $G$ is the maximum density $\rho(H)$ amongst all subgraphs $H\subseteq G$.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Computing maximum subgraph density has been studied both in the dynamic~\cite{chekuri2024adaptive,sawlani2020near , streaming~\cite{BahmaniKV12,BhattacharyaHNT15 and distributed~\cite{SarmaLNT12,SuVu20 setting.}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Often these measures are used to parameterise the sparsity of ``uniformly sparse graphs''~\cite{Behnezhadetal,Brodal99dynamicrepresentations,Onaketal .}
elements.sent-2411-023: These measures are global measures in the sense that they measure the sparsity of the most dense part of the graph.
elements.sent-2411-024: In many cases the graph is not equally sparse (or dense) everywhere.
elements.sent-2411-025: Consider for example a lollipop graph: a large clique joined to a long path.
elements.sent-2411-026: The clique is a subgraph of high density, yet the vertices along the path sit in a part of the graph that is significantly less dense.
elements.sent-2411-027: Often, solutions for graph density related problems provide guarantees based on the most dense part of the graph.
elements.sent-2411-028: In some areas of computation more ``local'' solutions are desirable.
elements.sent-2411-029: Prior works of a global nature often completely disregard certain parts of the graphs, meaning that the output in sparser parts holds little to no information.
elements.sent-2411-030: We give three examples: $1)$ Many dynamic algorithms for estimating the subgraph density rely on modifying the solution locally.
elements.sent-2411-031: Algorithmic performance is expressed in terms of (global) graph sparsity, and thus fails to exploit the more fine-grained guarantee that local sparse areas yield.
elements.sent-2411-032: However, since many classical algorithms are tuned towards only detecting the densest subgraphs, these algorithms might fail to detect communities in sparser parts of the network~\cite{Prac2,Prac1,Prac3 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: We consider the models LOCAL and CONGEST, and the lollipop graph.
elements.sent-2411-034: Here, almost instantly, the vertices of the clique realise they are part of a (very dense) clique.
elements.sent-2411-035: The vertices on the path may have to wait for diameter-many rounds before realising the maximum subgraph density of the graph.
elements.sent-2411-036: Distributed algorithms that wish to compute the value of the subgraph density are thus posed with a choice: either use $\Omega(D)$ rounds (where $D$ is the diameter of the graph), or let every vertex output a value that is at most the maximum subgraph density.
elements.sent-2411-037: We consider the definition of local density $\rho^*(v)$ by Danisch, Chan, and Sozio~\cite{Prac2 , defined at each node $v$ of the graph (Definition~\ref{def:localdensity ).}}

elements.sent-2411-040: We show that, just as in the maximum-subgraph density problem, computing local density has a natural dual problem as we define the local out-degree.

elements.sent-2411-044: We define this value $\gee^*(v)$ as the local out-degree of $v$.
elements.sent-2411-045: We prove that the local density of each vertex is the dual of its local out-degree and thereby $\gee^*(v) = \rho^*(v)$.

elements.algorithm-2411-002: [H] \caption{hour($h$)} \label{alg:hour} \begin{algorithmic} \FOR{$m = 0$ to $2 \lceil \eta^{-1} \rceil + 2$} \IF{ $m$ is even} \STATE evenminute($h$, $m$) \ELSE \STATE oddminute($h$, $m$) \ENDIF \ENDFOR \IF{$h > 0$} \STATE hour$(h-1)$ \ENDIF \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-003: [H] \caption{evenminute(int $h$, int $m$)} \label{alg:evenminute} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE $V_m := \{ v \in L_h \mid v \textnormal{ has at least one violating out-edge } \}$ \FOR{ $t = 0$ to $2 \lceil \log_{8/7} n \rceil + 1$} \STATE Let $A_t \subset V_m$ be the set of vertices at level $h$ with at least one violating out-edge \STATE Each $a \in A_t$ determines the set $E_t(a)$ of violating out-edges. \STATE Each $a \in A_t$ computes $\delta_t(a) = \gee(a) - (1 + \eta)^{h - 1}$ and reports $\delta_t(a) / |E_t(a)|$ across $E_t(a)$. \tcc{next round:} \STATE Let $B_t$ be the set of vertices that receive at least one violating in-edge from $A_t$. \STATE Each $b \in B_t$ determines the set $I_t(b)$ of vertices that reported a value to $v$. \STATE Each $b \in B_t$ sorts the $ a \in I_t(b)$ by $\delta(a)$. \STATE Each $b \in B_t$ greedily decreases $\gee(a \tor b)$ by at most $\delta_t(a)$ for $a \in I_t(b)$; until $\gee(b) = (1 + \frac{\eta}{2})^h$. \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-004: [H] \caption{oddminute(int $h$, int $m$)} \label{alg:minute} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE $T_m := \{v \in V_{m-1} \mid v \textnormal{ has at least one violating in-edge } \}$ \FOR{ $s = \ell$ down to $h$} \STATE second$(h, m ,s)$ \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-005: [H] \caption{second(int $h$, int $m$, int $s$)} \label{alg:second} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE Compute the graph $D_s$ in $\ell$ rounds. \STATE Compute the graph $D_s^*$ by adding a shared source to $S_s$ and a shared sink to $T_m$. \STATE $f =$ ComputeBlockingFlow($D_s^*$) \STATE Flip all edges in $D_s$ with the corresponding flow in $f$. \end{algorithmic}
